script:
Hello, I am Josh Weinberg and this is The Portfolio Podcast. Friday's market close — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed the session up one point two eight percent, a gain of one thousand two hundred eighty-three dollars and fifty-four cents. Current portfolio value stands at one hundred one thousand two hundred eighty-three dollars and fifty-four cents against a one hundred thousand dollar base.

Apple surged six point six six percent at Friday's close. That single move, the largest percentage gain among our nine holdings, nearly single-handedly drove the portfolio into the green. One stock. One session. That is what concentration risk — and concentration reward — looks like in real time.

The broad market finished the shortened pre-holiday session on firm footing, with technology leading the advance and the Nasdaq Composite closing higher by roughly one point five percent on the session. Sector rotation favored large-cap growth, with communication services and select consumer discretionary names participating. No major macro data points were released Friday ahead of the July Fourth holiday weekend, leaving price action driven primarily by stock-specific news flow and lighter-than-average holiday volume.

The top story of Friday's session belongs to Microsoft, M-S-F-T. The stock gained four point six eight percent, closing at three hundred ninety dollars and forty-nine cents, but the headline that matters most is what the company announced at Friday's open. Microsoft launched its new Microsoft Frontier Company initiative, committing two point five billion dollars to a dedicated enterprise artificial intelligence adoption unit. The division will embed six thousand AI engineers and specialists directly inside enterprise customer organizations, working alongside consulting partners including Accenture and KPMG. This is not a product update. It is a structural expansion of Microsoft's AI revenue architecture, moving from selling software licenses to embedding human capital directly into client operations — a model that carries meaningfully higher contract values and stickier retention. The announcement comes after a brutal June for the stock. A separate report noted that Microsoft declined nearly twenty percent in June, its worst single month in more than twenty-five years, a drawdown that took the stock back to levels not seen since the post-rate-hike compression of twenty twenty-two. For QQQM holders, Friday's move and the Frontier Company announcement together suggest institutional money began rebuilding positions into the holiday weekend. The question now is whether the two point five billion dollar commitment translates into accelerating revenue guidance when Microsoft reports earnings later this quarter.

Turning now to the full holdings rundown, starting with the largest positions and working down.

Apple, A-A-P-L, closed at three hundred eight dollars and sixty-three cents, up nineteen dollars and twenty-seven cents, a gain of six point six six percent on the session. That is the standout move of the day across the entire portfolio. Two separate catalysts appear to be driving the surge. First, Investor's Business Daily flagged Apple and Robinhood as flashing buy signals on Friday, noting that the broader tech rally signals the artificial intelligence trend is not over. Second, a widely-circulated report noted that Apple is now approximately four percent away from overtaking Nvidia, N-V-D-A, as the world's most valuable company by market capitalization — a gap that, per the analysis, a single earnings report could close. There is also a more complicated story running underneath the surface. Reports indicate Apple is exploring a partnership with Chinese memory supplier CXMT, a move that rattled Western Digital shares by nearly ten percent on Friday. The CXMT angle introduces supply chain optionality for Apple — potentially lower-cost memory inputs — but it also carries geopolitical sensitivity given ongoing U.S.-China technology trade tensions. Watch the CXMT partnership headline closely. Any Commerce Department response could create headline risk for A-A-P-L heading into earnings.

Microsoft, M-S-F-T, as covered in the top story, closed at three hundred ninety dollars and forty-nine cents, up four point six eight percent. The Frontier Company launch is the primary catalyst. At eight point four two percent of the portfolio, M-S-F-T is the second-largest holding, and Friday's recovery matters after a historically poor June. The next concrete catalyst is the upcoming earnings report. Guidance language around Frontier Company revenue contribution will be critical.

Nvidia, N-V-D-A, closed at one hundred ninety-four dollars and eighty-three cents, down five dollars and twenty-six cents, a loss of two point six three percent on the session. The decline is notable given the broad tech rally that lifted most of the portfolio. No direct company-specific news drove the move Friday. The available news tagged to N-V-D-A covers Berkshire Hathaway's record cash position, cryptocurrency speculation, and general market valuation warnings — none of it Nvidia-specific. The underperformance relative to the Nasdaq on a strong session is worth monitoring. At eight point one five percent of the portfolio, any sustained divergence between Nvidia and the broader AI trade carries meaningful portfolio impact. Watch for any analyst commentary on data center order flow or export license developments over the coming sessions.

Over at Alphabet, G-O-O-G-L, the stock closed at three hundred fifty-nine dollars and ninety-one cents, up two dollars and fifty-four cents, a modest gain of zero point seven one percent. Two news items are relevant. First, Anthropic has entered early talks with Samsung to manufacture a custom AI chip, according to Bloomberg. Anthropic has historically relied on chips rented from Amazon, Google, and Nvidia. If Anthropic moves toward custom silicon, it reduces dependence on Google's cloud infrastructure — a modest but real headwind for Google Cloud revenue at the margin. Second, a broader report on AI and workforce transformation noted the emergence of high-paying AI-adjacent trade roles, a macro theme that supports enterprise AI spending broadly. G-O-O-G-L remains an eight point zero one percent position. The Anthropic chip story is early-stage, but it is worth tracking as a potential cloud revenue risk.

Amazon, A-M-Z-N, closed at two hundred forty-two dollars and sixty-seven cents, up four dollars and thirty-three cents, a gain of one point eight two percent. No Amazon-specific headlines drove the move Friday. The gain aligns with the broader consumer discretionary and cloud technology recovery. At five point five two percent of the portfolio, A-M-Z-N is the fifth-largest holding. The Anthropic chip development noted in the Alphabet discussion is indirectly relevant here as well — Amazon is Anthropic's primary cloud infrastructure provider, and any shift in Anthropic's chip strategy touches Amazon Web Services economics. Next earnings report remains the primary near-term catalyst.

Broadcom, A-V-G-O, closed at three hundred sixty dollars and forty-five cents, down seventeen dollars and thirty cents, a loss of four point five eight percent on the session. That is a sharp decline on a day when the broader technology sector was broadly higher. No specific news was available for A-V-G-O Friday. The move likely reflects semiconductor sector rotation, with money flowing toward megacap software and away from fabless chip names amid ongoing uncertainty around AI infrastructure spending pacing. At four point six one percent of the portfolio, the drawdown is meaningful. Watch for any analyst updates on custom AI chip order visibility, which is Broadcom's primary near-term growth driver.

Meanwhile, Meta Platforms, M-E-T-A, closed at five hundred eighty-two dollars and ninety cents, up nineteen dollars and sixty-one cents, a gain of three point four eight percent. No Meta-specific headlines were available Friday. The gain is consistent with the communication services sector participating in the broader risk-on session. At four point four eight percent of the portfolio, M-E-T-A continues to trade in line with the large-cap AI beneficiary theme. Llama model development updates and advertising revenue guidance remain the key catalysts to monitor heading into earnings season.

Tesla, T-S-L-A, closed at three hundred ninety-three dollars and forty-five cents, down twenty-seven dollars and fifteen cents, a decline of six point four six percent. That is the largest percentage loss in the portfolio on Friday and stands in stark contrast to the broader market rally. No Tesla-specific news was available in Friday's feed. The severity of the drop on a strong tape suggests either institutional distribution or sector-specific pressure in consumer discretionary. At three point five four percent of the portfolio, T-S-L-A adds meaningful volatility. Delivery data and any production guidance updates are the near-term metrics to monitor.

Finally, Costco, C-O-S-T, closed at nine hundred fifty-one dollars and sixty-seven cents, up sixteen dollars and twenty cents, a gain of one point seven three percent. No Costco-specific news was available Friday. The consumer staples position continued its steady performance, consistent with its role as a relative stability anchor in the portfolio. At two point eight one percent, C-O-S-T is the smallest of the nine holdings covered. The next same-store sales report is the primary fundamental catalyst to watch.

Three numbers to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any Commerce Department response to the Apple and CXMT memory partnership report — given existing U.S.-China chip restrictions, a regulatory signal could move A-A-P-L sharply. Second, institutional positioning data from Friday's holiday-shortened session, which will clarify whether the Microsoft Frontier Company announcement triggered genuine accumulation or a one-day technical bounce. Third, any updated analyst price targets on Nvidia following its underperformance on a strong tape — the divergence from the AI rally is an early-warning signal worth quantifying.

That is Friday's close for the QQQM portfolio. The next episode drops at five PM Eastern on the next trading day. Watch the Apple and Nvidia divergence closely — one is approaching a market cap record, the other is showing the first cracks in its AI leadership trade. I am Josh Weinberg. This has been The Portfolio Podcast.
